cataloging_subject.label: Isopes Fabules (Fables I-IV and VII)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is one Middle English seven-fable work by John Lydgate, represented here by Zupitza's edited selection of Fables I-IV from Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and Fable VII from Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 59. Fable IV is a three-stanza witness fragment. Fables V and VI are absent from the XML. Zupitza's German preface and notes belong to the source representation, not to the medieval work's language, form, or extent; manuscripts, editions, encodings, facsimiles, and the mediated Ashmole transcript remain distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Isopes Fabules
catalog_summary.author: John Lydgate
catalog_summary.date: Possibly before 1400; exact composition date and unity unresolved
catalog_summary.region: England; Oxford is securely associated only with Fable VII's manuscript rubric

titles.supplied_by: Harley MS 2251 catalog and DIMEV normalized title

titles.value: Wisdom is more of price than gold in coffers
notes: Normalized discovery form; witness spellings vary.

titles.value: Here begynneþe a notable proverbe of ysopus Ethiopyene in balad by dann Johan Lidegate made in Oxenford
notes: The Oxford claim is scoped to this rubric and must not be transferred automatically to the entire seven-fable collection.

agents.name: Julius Zupitza
notes: Editor of the 1890 immediate source article.

agents.name: Arthur Sampson Napier
notes: Zupitza names only his friend Napier. Same-volume Oxford attribution to A. Napier and later authority evidence make the full-name identification strongly supported but inferential.
The collection adapts Aesopic material; its precise source exemplars and routes remain unresolved.

scope.description: 1890 immediate source article and its selected fables
Selected text collated against corresponding Harley MS 2251 readings
Working transcription of MS Ashmole 59, ff. 24v-25r supplied to Zupitza

notes: The surname is explicit; the full-name resolution is inferential.
Wheatley reports clues that may suggest a date before 1400 but rejects unsupported precision; manuscript dates are not composition dates.

date_statements.display: 1460-1490 for the represented Trinity copy of Fables I-IV
1447-1456 for Ashmole 59 Part I; Wheatley dates the Fable VII copying to 1455/56

notes: The Ashmole rubric says this fable was made at Oxford; it does not securely locate the entire collection.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The encoding also contains Zupitza's German editorial prose and notes, which are outside the abstract medieval work and therefore not cataloged as a work language.

form_statements.description: Rhyme royal stanzas, normally seven decasyllabic lines with ababbcc rhyme. The represented selection has 82 seven-line stanza groups and 574 lines.

summaries.value: John Lydgate's Isopes Fabules recasts Aesopic animal narratives in rhyme royal, expanding brief tales into moral arguments about wisdom, sufficiency, tyranny, deceit, and social justice. CME00105 preserves five selected textual units: the cock and precious stone, wolf and lamb, frog and mouse, a three-stanza fragment of the hound and sheep, and the hound with stolen cheese. The first four derive directly from Trinity R.3.19; the last reaches Zupitza through a transcription supplied by Arthur Sampson Napier from Ashmole 59. Harley 2251, the only medieval witness containing all seven fables, supplies collateral readings.

extent.description: The cataloged medieval selection has five fable sections, 82 LG elements, and 574 L elements; each verse group contains seven lines. Fables I-IV have 224, 133, 168, and 21 lines, and Fable VII has 28 independently numbered lines. The XML additionally has one German prose preface, 318 editorial notes, 223 manuscript-folio milestones, and 20 PB elements for printed pp. 6-25; those representation metrics are not added to the medieval-work extent.

content_parts.label: Fable I: The Cock and the Precious Stone
content_parts.summary: A cock rejects a jewel he cannot use; the expanded moral values wisdom over material wealth and includes the collection prologue and a twenty-one-line envoy.

content_parts.label: Fable II: The Wolf and the Lamb
content_parts.summary: A wolf manufactures accusations against an innocent lamb and kills it, supporting a moral critique of tyranny and false pretexts.

content_parts.label: Fable III: The Frog and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: A deceitful frog tries to drown a mouse; a kite takes both, turning treachery against its perpetrator.

content_parts.label: Fable IV: The Hound and the Sheep (fragment)
content_parts.summary: The surviving three stanzas begin a false-witness dispute in which a hound makes a fraudulent claim against a sheep.
notes: Trinity R.3.19 stops after three stanzas; the abstract fable is not thereby a twenty-one-line complete work.

content_parts.label: Fable VII: The Hound and the Cheese
content_parts.summary: A dog carrying stolen cheese sees its reflection, grasps for the apparent second piece, and loses what it had; the moral condemns covetousness and ill-gotten gain.
notes: Zupitza printed this text from a working transcription supplied by Napier, not from a claimed direct inspection of Ashmole 59.

related_works.title: Fables
notes: Modern scholarship identifies Marie's Fables among the closest sources, but the exact exemplar and route of transmission are unresolved.

related_works.title: Aesopica / Romulus tradition
notes: The Latin Romulus tradition is a close source complex; this record does not claim one identified physical exemplar.

evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD, IDG, TEXT, and complete BODY
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, selection boundary, five fable divisions, titles, language, lineation, notes, printed pages, and manuscript-folio milestones.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XML element, division, pagination, and milestone audit of CME00105.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY direct and nested element counts
evidence.summary: Confirms five DIV1, five nested DIV2, 82 LG, 574 L, one P, 318 NOTE, 20 PB numbered 6-25, and exact per-fable line and stanza counts; no L or LG occurs inside NOTE.

evidence.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Zu Lydgates Isopus,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 85 (1890): 1-28.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 1-28 / PDF pp. 17-44; encoded selection pp. 6-25
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for source witnesses, editorial methods, direct Trinity transcription, Harley collation, Napier-mediated Ashmole transcription, and exact source and encoding boundaries.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, record 6701.
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence that the seven fables form one full record, with title, author, incipit, rhyme royal form, three witnesses, exact loci, and bibliography.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, manuscript R.3.19 catalog and IIIF manifest.
evidence.summary: Official evidence for shelfmark, codex description, target fable sequence and fragment, provenance, and the exact public IIIF object and license.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS. Ashmole 59 catalog TEI, manuscript_356.
evidence.summary: Authoritative evidence for the composite codex, John Shirley's 1447-1456 first part, exact four-stanza Fable VII locus, rubric, language, and imperfection. A fixed local copy is recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2251, record 040-002048082.
evidence.summary: Current evidence for the complete seven-fable witness, work title and incipit, London origin, 1460-1483 date, Hammond Scribe attribution, locus, DIMEV identifiers, and currently unavailable legacy digital images.

evidence.citation: Edward Wheatley, ed., Isopes Fabules, Middle English Texts Series (Medieval Institute Publications, 2013).
evidence.locator: complete 47-page PDF; introduction, text, notes, and bibliography
evidence.summary: Modern editorial evidence for date and unity cautions, sources, form, themes, three-witness transmission, witness dates and defects, and the limited scope of the Oxford rubric.

evidence.citation: Arthur Sampson Napier identification audit: same-volume 'A. Napier' Oxford signature, later bibliographic normalization, and authority records.
evidence.summary: Strongly supports resolving Zupitza's unnamed friend Napier as Oxford scholar Arthur Sampson Napier while preserving the inferential nature of the full-name identification.

open_questions.question: When and where were the seven fables composed, in what order, and how secure is their authorial unity?
next_steps: Consult post-2013 scholarship and collate all three witnesses.
Keep the Oxford rubric scoped to Fable VII.
Distinguish DIMEV's useful one-work catalog boundary from proof of authorial assembly or order.

open_questions.question: Which physical exemplars and textual routes from Marie de France, Gualterus Anglicus, and the Romulus tradition underlie each fable?
next_steps: Compare each fable against the proposed French and Latin sources.
Do not collapse source-family proximity into one identified exemplar.

open_questions.question: Does Arthur Sampson Napier's working transcription of Ashmole 59 survive, and can its exact mediation of Zupitza's Fable VII text be reconstructed?
next_steps: Search Zupitza and Napier papers and correspondence.
Compare Zupitza's text with a new image-based transcription of Ashmole 59, ff. 24v-25r.

open_questions.question: Can complete public or repository-supplied facsimiles of Ashmole 59 and Harley 2251 be obtained, and what exact reuse terms apply to them and to the U-M encoding?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian and the British Library first about existing complete surrogates, then request whole-object quotations if needed.
Recheck the BL legacy digitization and U-M item after service barriers clear.
Record image-level publication and reuse terms without transferring licenses between layers.

notes: DIMEV record 6701 is the decisive catalog boundary: one seven-fable abstract work represented by a five-fable edited selection, not five unrelated works.

DIMEV's Harley source note appears to describe only four stanzas of Fable VII even though the current BL catalog, the record's full locus and boundary lines, and Wheatley support a complete seven-fable Harley witness; the discrepancy is retained as a review caveat.
The German prose preface and 318 notes are source-representation features and are excluded from the medieval work's 574-line extent.
